title: practical english usage - Part 339
content:
T  hese forms are more common in British English; in American English have to / but not You need fill-in-a form; I wonder if I need fill in a form.  This is the only form you need fill in.

You needn't fill in a form.     Need I fill in a form?

vocabulary' •  533   next and the next; nearest and (2) on Sunday week, a week on Sunday or (on) Sunday next week.

the!this Sunday coming, the!this coming Sunday or (on) Sunday this week, To  avoid misunderstanding, one can say for example (1) on Sunday, this Sunday, ‘ See you next Sunday.' ‘ Do you mean this coming Sunday or the one after?' exactly what is meant.

When  next is used with the names of days or months, it is not always clear 3   next     Sunday,        etc The next few days will be wet.

We   generally say the next few days, not the-next day not I'll be at college for the next three years, (     ...

Note the word order in expressions with numbers.

2 the    next     three      ..., etc NOW    Thurs           Fri   Sat   Sun       Mon        Tu     Wed     Thurs           Fri   Sat   Sun       FUTURE the next week ►    NOW    Thurs           Fri   Sat   Sun       Mon         Tu     Wed     Thurs           Fri   Sat   Sun       FUTURE next week The  difference between last and the last is similar, ►    505.

The next year will be difficult. (= the twelve months starting now) - Next year will be difficult. (= the year starting next January) I'll be busy for the next week. (= the seven days starting today) not - Goodbye - see you next week, (     .. .-see you-the-next week.) Compare: July 15th to August 15th; the next year is the period from July 2016  to July 2017.

moment of speaking. On July 15th 2016, the next month is the period from The next week, month, etc is the period of seven, thirty, etc days starting at the If I am speaking in July, next month is August; in 2016, next year is 2017.

Next week, month, etc (without the) is the week, month, etc just after this one.

1 next     week,      month,       etc;   the    next     week,      month,       etc 533 next     and   t he   ne  xt;  nearest For there is no need to. .  ►    20.

‘ She looks quite ill. J'm sure it's flu.' ‘ It needn't be - maybe she's just Need not is also sometimes used to say that something is not necessarily true.

You mustn't tell Charlotte - I don't want her to know.

You needn't tell Jennifer - she already knows.

Word  Problems from A  to Z     Section 31 534 vocabulary' •         no doubt Annie doesn't live here anymore.

Anymore may be written as one word, especially in American English.

This can't go on any longer. I'm not helping you any more.

I no longer support the Conservative party, (     I no more-support...) the verb), not... any longer or not... any more.

idea of actions and situations stopping. Instead, we  use no longer (usually before We   do not use no more in standard modern English as an adverb to express the There's no more bread.

We   use no more with nouns to talk about quantity or degree - to say how  much.

not    any     longer 535  no   more,      not    any     more,      no   longer, For structures with the verb doubt, ►    436.

there is no doubt that.

Doubtless is similar to no doubt (but more formal); undoubtedly is similar to lhan driving.) not    o Cycling is certainly healthier than driving, (     N  doubt cycling is healthier gelling warmer.) not    o There is no doubt that the world is getting warmer, (     N  doubt the world is without any doubt (formal), certainly, definitely.

To  say that something is certain, we  can use there is no doubt that (formal), You're tired, no doubt. I'll make you a cup of tea.

No doubt it'll rain soon.

not mean ‘ certainly'.

No doubt expresses a personal opinion. It means'probably' or 'I suppose’ ; it does 534 no   doubt ► and         ʒɜɑ no       not Come and sit next to me.

Next to means ‘ beside'.

The people at the next table were having a terrible argument.

My girlfriend lives next door. Who works in the next office?

Next can also be used to talk about the nearest position in a row.
